mappings.rationaleAI: Learning is the direct subject of Section 342's B division, covering non-associative learning such as habituation and sensitization (B.1), associative learning including classical and operant conditioning (B.2), and observational and play-based learning (B.4).

Intelligence covers complex problem-solving capacities including abstraction, reasoning, and adaptive behavior in novel environments (B.5), discrimination of relational stimuli (B.5a), and insight and reasoning (B.5c), directly addressing Section 342's higher behavioral characteristics.

Human behavior covers the full range of behavioral responses — social, cognitive, moral, developmental, and group behavior — spanning the basic behavioral activities of individuals (A), higher cognitive behavioral characteristics (B), and group behaviour dynamics (C).

While the article focuses on social discrimination, the cognitive capacity to make distinctions among categories of stimuli — discriminations of relational and abstract stimuli — directly maps to outline path B.5a, the only uncovered priority path in this section most closely addressed by this candidate.
Communication covers animal, plant, fungal, and interspecies signaling — directly matching C.2 (animal communication) as a core component of group behaviour in Section 342.

Language covers acquisition and structure of linguistic systems — directly relevant to B.5d (language learning in animals and humans) and C.2 (animal communication via vocalizations and other signals).

Ecology covers habitat, ecological niche, niche construction, and the influence of physical environmental factors on organisms — directly relevant to A.5 (behaviour related to habitat), a priority path with no other recommendations.

Primates demonstrate advanced complex problem-solving including tool use and complex vocalizations (B.5, B.5c), and their social structures — ranging from pair bonds to multi-male groups — illustrate the range of social behaviour among animals (C.3).

Play covers its functions in cognitive development, observational and social learning (B.4), and social bonding (C.3), and documents play behaviour across mammals and birds as a basic behavioral activity of individual animals (A); the article connects biological drive and social learning contexts.

Thought covers insight, reasoning, problem-solving, deliberation, and concept formation — directly mapping to B.5c (insight and reasoning) in the complex problem-solving portion of the higher behavioral characteristics division.

Memory as encoding, storage, and retrieval of information underlies spatial learning and navigation (B.3) and is foundational to associative learning processes (B.2), making it central to the higher behavioral characteristics of individual animals.

Mind covers the attribution of reasoning, insight, and complex cognitive processes to both humans and non-human animals, directly addressing B.5c (insight and reasoning) as well as the broader question of mental capacities in non-human species.

Friendship as a voluntary dyadic relationship with mutual affection, reciprocity, and social support illustrates the range (C.3) and dynamics (C.4) of social behaviour, including evolutionary social behaviour frameworks referenced in the article.

Community covers social grouping, identity formation, socialisation, and collective function — directly mapping to the behaviour of animals in groups (C), distinctions among social group types (C.3), and social behaviour dynamics (C.4).

Emotion covers behavioral responses including fear-based avoidance and aggressive states (A.4 — aggressive behaviour) and its role in social interaction dynamics (C.4), with evolutionary accounts connecting emotion to adaptive behaviour in social contexts.

The cat article covers predatory food-getting behaviour (A.1), avoidance behaviour as a solitary hunter (A.3), aggressive and defensive threat displays (A.4), and problem-solving capacity (B.5), spanning multiple basic behavioral activities of individual animals.

Reason covers deductive, inductive, abductive, and analogical reasoning as cognitive capacities — directly mapping to outline path B.5c (insight and reasoning) in the complex problem-solving section of higher behavioral characteristics.

Toys support observational and imitation-based learning in children and animals through play, directly relevant to B.4 (perceptual learning: imitation and observational learning), and the developmental psychology context aligns with learning in early individual development.

Sport involves organized competition (A.4 — aggressive behaviour in structured contest) and rule-governed group activity (C.3 — range of social behaviour), providing a human-specific example of competitive and social behavioral patterns.

Child development covers the progressive maturation of cognitive, physical, emotional, and social behavior across early, middle, and late childhood — encompassing developmental changes in both basic behavioral activities (A) and higher behavioral characteristics (B) of individual young animals.

Psychology systematically covers perception, cognition, learning, motivation, and social behaviour — the scientific framework underlying both the higher behavioral characteristics of individuals (B) and group and social behaviour (C) outlined in Section 342.

Adolescence covers developmental gains in abstract and hypothetical reasoning, metacognition, and autonomous behaviour (B.5 — complex problem solving) alongside maturation of basic behavioral capacities including reproductive and social behaviour (A.6, A broadly).
